contract.title: SCOTUS rules Trump can fire FTC commissioners?
contract.resolution_criteria:
This market will resolve to 'Yes' if the U.S. Supreme Court rules in Trump v.
Slaughter (or a consolidated companion case) that the President may remove Federal
Trade Commission commissioners without cause, by December 31, 2026, 11:59 PM ET.
Resolution requires a majority opinion from the Supreme Court. Otherwise, this
market will resolve to 'No'.

Background: President Trump fired FTC Commissioners Rebecca Kelly Slaughter and
Alvaro Bedoya in early 2025. The case challenges the 1935 Humphrey's Executor
precedent, which prohibits presidents from removing commissioners of independent
agencies without cause. A ruling for Trump would fundamentally restructure the
FTC's independence from executive control and reshape antitrust enforcement authority.

The Supreme Court's term ends in June 2026; a decision is expected before then.
Market currently prices a 97% probability of YES.

settlement_entities.name: Supreme Court of the United States

conditions.label: SCOTUS majority in Trump v. Slaughter
conditions.summary: By Dec 31, 2026, SCOTUS issues a majority opinion in Trump v. Slaughter holding the President may remove FTC commissioners without cause.

conditions.label: SCOTUS majority in companion case
conditions.summary: By Dec 31, 2026, SCOTUS issues a majority opinion in a consolidated companion case holding the President may remove FTC commissioners without cause.

contract.narrative: From Dec 2025 to Apr 2026, the FTC remains active with merger remedies, consent orders, consumer-protection actions, and administrative filings, none of which bear on presidential removal authority. These are background signals with no directional effect; the key legal question remains pending as the market awaits a Supreme Court ruling on whether Trump can fire FTC commissioners.

primary_drivers: Major FTC complaints and injunctions (e.g., complaint for permanent injunction)
High‑profile merger reviews/blocks (OkCupid/Match; healthcare merger intervention)
Broad enforcement probes and consent orders (student‑loan operator stop; no‑hire consent order)
heat_panel.explainer: Critical heat reflects a cluster of high‑impact FTC enforcement actions and litigation this quarter — major complaints, injunctions, consent orders, merger interventions, and broad investigations.
